考研英语外刊精读

How Tech Reporting Moved Into the Physical World

科技报道如何走进物理世界：AI 数据中心背后的现实叩问

来源：The Guardian 原文日期：July 12, 2026 精读生成：2026-07-12 话题：科技 / 社会 / 媒体

文章大意

本文出自《卫报》，介绍了科技报道如何从屏幕上的虚拟世界转向 AI 数据中心、能源基础设施等实体现场。记者们通过大量实地走访，揭示科技巨头在数据中心规划、能源承诺、就业创造等方面的现实约束，以及这些巨型设施对周边社区、环境和普通人生活的真实影响。文章还指出，随着社交媒体危害、青少年网络伤害等议题受到关注，科技记者的工作越来越需要“脚踏实地”的现场报道，而非仅依赖数字信息。

阅读技巧提示

  • 本文是新闻评论/记者工作随笔，结构较为松散，多引用不同记者的观点。建议先把握主线：科技报道为何从线上走向线下，再细读具体案例。
  • 注意比喻和习语：shoe-leather reporting 不是“皮鞋报道”，而是“实地采访、跑新闻”；wave a magic wand 指“挥动魔杖”，比喻轻而易举地实现某事。
  • 长难句多为直接引语，包含多个并列成分或插入语。阅读时可先找到说话人及其核心观点，再处理修饰成分。
  • 词义猜测题可借助上下文同义复现：如 physical constraints, reality checks, tangible stuff 在语义上相互呼应，都指向“现实限制”。

正文精读

Journalists often use the term shoe-leather reportingn. /ˈʃuː ˌleðər rɪˈpɔːrtɪŋ/
实地采访；跑新闻（比喻记者亲自走访、脚踏实地做报道） to refer to the on-the-ground legworkn. /ˈleɡwɜːrk/
外勤工作；跑腿调查 that goes into covering certain stories. As the tech industry’s focus has shifted from screen-based realities to the physical world of colossaladj. /kəˈlɒsl/
巨大的；庞大的 AI datacentres and social media harms, comfortable footwear has become more essential to a tech reporter’s job.

Earlier this week, we published the Guardian’s latest investigation into the datacentres and energy infrastructures that underpinv. /ˌʌndərˈpɪn/
支撑；巩固；构成……的基础 AI – revealing that an £8.2bn AI complex in rural Scotland has misrepresentedv. /ˌmɪsreprɪˈzentɪd/
歪曲；虚报；误导 its plans to be powered entirely by on-siteadj. /ˌɒnˈsaɪt/
现场的；就地发生的 renewablesn. /rɪˈnjuːəblz/
可再生能源. "Our reporting is showing that you can’t simply wave a magic wandphr. /weɪv ə ˈmædʒɪk wɒnd/
挥动魔杖；轻而易举地解决 and have a datacentre appear," says Aisha Down, who covers AI for the Guardian and went to Scotland to investigate the story. "There are a lot of huge physical constraintsn. /kənˈstreɪnts/
限制；约束 and reality checks. These physical, tangibleadj. /ˈtændʒəbl/
有形的；可触摸的；实实在在的 things are what makes or sinks the AI boom."

Much of this reporting involves interrogatingv. /ɪnˈterəɡeɪtɪŋ/
审问；质疑；深入调查 the reality of all this tangible stuff – whether or not the infrastructure proposals by tech giants are realistic, whether the datacentres themselves are feasibleadj. /ˈfiːzəbl/
可行的；行得通的, whether the energy and water commitments are genuineadj. /ˈdʒenjuɪn/
真实的；真诚的, whether the promised job creation is real and, above all, what it means for real-life, actual people.

The datacentre investigation published this week illustrates the growing intersectionn. /ˌɪntərˈsekʃn/
交叉；交集 of tech reporting with energy and environment stories. It involved Aisha trekkingv. /ˈtrekɪŋ/
长途跋涉；艰难行进 around Lanarkshire to sites where datacentres and energy infrastructure might be built, talking with local residents, examining public records and obtaining internal documents. It followed a similar investigation earlier this year in which she checked out a four-acre site on the outskirtsn. /ˈaʊtskɜːrts/
郊区；市郊 of London that is due to house a gleamingadj. /ˈɡliːmɪŋ/
闪闪发光的；崭新的 AI supercomputern. /ˈsuːpərkəmpjuːtər/
超级计算机 complex – only to find that it was still being used as a scaffolding yard.

And, yes, both stories involved a lot of "shoe-leather reporting" – although, technically, Aisha wasn’t wearing leather-soled shoes: "I wear very light flats with rubber soles and a band that makes them good for walking in. In Lanarkshire, I wore sneakers."

Dan Milmo, our global technology editor (who generally wears rubber-soled chukka bootsn. /ˈtʃʌkə buːts/
查卡靴（一种及踝短靴）), published an article about the number of large datacentre projects around the world that are being challenged or cancelled. "I remember going out to a site in Wales that was about as well-organised and well-funded as you can get, and I still got a sense of how difficult it is for tech companies to pull offphr. /pʊl ɒf/
成功完成；实现 these big infrastructure projects," he says.

The physical reality checks on AI include the capacity of local electricity gridsn. /ɡrɪdz/
电网；网格, the availability of chips and other componentsn. /kəmˈpoʊnənts/
组成部分；零部件, as well as the impact on tech companies’ carbon footprintsn. /ˈkɑːrbən ˈfʊtprɪnts/
碳足迹 and sustainabilityn. /səˌsteɪnəˈbɪləti/
可持续性 goals. "The AI boom has radicallyadv. /ˈrædɪkli/
根本地；彻底地 changed the physical presence of all of these tech companies in the physical world," says Blake Montgomery, the Guardian’s US tech editor (who does wear a pair of leather-soled shoes). "These AI datacentres are some of the most massiveadj. /ˈmæsɪv/
巨大的；大规模的 and complexadj. /ˈkɒmpleks/
复杂的 structures that humanity has created. So we’re now also reporting on physical infrastructure and real places that exist, not just the digital landscapes in the more social media-focused era of tech reporting."

The sheeradj. /ʃɪər/
纯粹的；十足的；极其巨大的 scale of these developments is just one aspect of the physicalityn. /ˌfɪzɪˈkæləti/
实体性；物质性 facing tech journalists today. Another is the sensoryadj. /ˈsensəri/
感官的；感觉的 assaultn. /əˈsɔːlt/
攻击；冲击. Last month, Aisha visited Slough, home of the largest datacentre park in Europe, to experience the swelteringadj. /ˈsweltərɪŋ/
酷热的；热得难受的 heat-island effectn. /hiːt ˈaɪlənd ɪˈfekt/
热岛效应 – with some research suggesting that temperatures in the immediate vicinityn. /vəˈsɪnəti/
附近；邻近地区 of datacentres can increase by an average of 2C, and as much as 9C. "It was baking hot and there was this audible whine. If you were sleeping or working nearby everyday, I think it’d wear you down."

Robert Booth, our UK technology editor, experienced even louder volumes when reporting on mammothadj. /ˈmæməθ/
巨大的；庞大的 datacentres in Santa Clara in Silicon Valley. "They call those datacentres screamers because they’re louder than an aircraft taking off," he says. "I had to pack my ears with protection and, even with that, I was still left with ringing ears."

He says shoe-leather reporting is essentialadj. /ɪˈsenʃl/
必不可少的；极其重要的 for covering the AI revolutionn. /ˌrevəˈluːʃn/
革命；剧变. "It’s only really possible by getting out and reporting on the pace of change, the tensionsn. /ˈtenʃnz/
紧张；张力；矛盾 it creates, how it impacts people. I’ve also gone to kids’ clubs to see how children are grappling withphr. /ˈɡræplɪŋ wɪð/
努力应对；努力理解 AI. It’s a really important part of the job to make sure we’re reporting from somewhere rather than from the digital ethern. /ˈiːθər/
以太；虚空；太空," he says. "But, no, I haven’t needed to alter my footwear."

Blake recallsv. /rɪˈkɔːlz/
回忆；回想起 sending tech reporter Dara Kerr to the Nevada desert, home of one of the biggest datacentre complexes in the world, for this visually stunningadj. /ˈstʌnɪŋ/
令人惊叹的；极好的 feature about their impact. "She basically got chased awayphr. /tʃeɪst əˈweɪ/
被赶走；被驱逐 by security," he says. (It’s unknown what footwear Dara was wearing at the time.)

Local tensions and backlashesn. /ˈbæklæʃɪz/
强烈反对；抵制 against datacentres are a big part of today’s tech story. Last week, we published an interview with Erin Brockovich about her work helping the many communities affected by the impact of datacentres on energy and water resources.

"Protests against datacentres are becoming a kind of manifestationn. /ˌmænɪfeˈsteɪʃn/
表现；显现 of voter and public concern about tech and AI in general," says Dan. "It’s very difficult for people to protest against technology or to make tangible their feelings about things that are transmitted over the internet. But while it’s hard to protest against ChatGPT and what it means for your undergraduate kids’ employment prospects, it’s easier to protest against the infrastructure that facilitatesv. /fəˈsɪlɪteɪts/
促进；使便利 its existence and how that infrastructure functions."

Dan notes that these real-world flashpointsn. /ˈflæʃpɔɪnts/
爆发点；导火索 are part of a bigger shift in the tech landscapen. /ˈlændskeɪp/
格局；形势；地貌. "When I started covering this industry in 2021, it was clear that these giant companies had so much money that they were just going to get bigger and more powerful and that the real-life impacts would simply grow. And inevitably my reporting has increasingly become about those impacts and the consequences of governments allowing these companies to grow without regulation."

He points to key turning points such as the inquestn. /ˈɪnkwest/
审讯；调查 into the death of Molly Russell, a London teenager who died from an act of self-harmn. /ˌself ˈhɑːrm/
自残；自我伤害 in 2017 after the darker side of online life overwhelmed her. In the US, tech reporters have also been attending courtroom hearings about social media’s physical and emotional harms, with further cases on the horizon about the platforms’ addictivenessn. /əˈdɪktɪvnəs/
成瘾性. Meanwhile, the UK has announced a bann. /bæn/
禁令；禁止 on social media for under-16s, following a ban introduced in Australia last year.

Last month, Dan went to west London to interview children and teenagers about the UK’s under-16 social media ban. "I was struck by how I hadn’t heard enough of those voices, and I personally hadn’t written enough about what these children think and how important their perspectiven. /pərˈspektɪv/
视角；观点 was," he says.

It’s a long way from the early days of social media, when many tech reporters were tasked with scrolling through their screens and downloading apps to spot the latest social media trend or internet buzz.

"Perhaps because unrealityn. /ˌʌnriˈæləti/
虚幻；不真实 has become so pervasiveadj. /pərˈveɪsɪv/
普遍的；无处不在的, reality has become a lot more interesting to people," Aisha suggests. "Did a child harm themselves because of their social media feed? How hot is it next to a datacentre? People are now more interested in those kinds of stories."

点击红色划线词汇可查看中文释义。

短语搭配

shoe-leather reporting
实地采访；跑新闻
"Journalists often use the term shoe-leather reporting to refer to the on-the-ground legwork that goes into covering certain stories."
wave a magic wand
挥动魔杖；轻而易举地实现
"Our reporting is showing that you can't simply wave a magic wand and have a datacentre appear."
on the outskirts of
在……的郊区
"...she checked out a four-acre site on the outskirts of London that is due to house a gleaming AI supercomputer complex."
pull off
成功完成；实现
"...I still got a sense of how difficult it is for tech companies to pull off these big infrastructure projects."
carbon footprint
碳足迹
"The physical reality checks on AI include... the impact on tech companies' carbon footprints and sustainability goals."
heat-island effect
热岛效应
"...Aisha visited Slough... to experience the sweltering heat-island effect."
grapple with
努力应对；努力理解
"I've also gone to kids' clubs to see how children are grappling with AI."
chase away
赶走；驱逐
"Blake recalls sending tech reporter Dara Kerr to the Nevada desert... She basically got chased away by security."

长难句解析

Much of this reporting involves interrogating the reality of all this tangible stuff – whether or not the infrastructure proposals by tech giants are realistic, whether the datacentres themselves are feasible, whether the energy and water commitments are genuine, whether the promised job creation is real and, above all, what it means for real-life, actual people.

结构分析：主干为 Much of this reporting involves interrogating the reality of all this tangible stuff；破折号后五个 whether/what 引导的从句为同位语，具体说明 tangible stuff 的内容；above all 为插入语，强调最后一点。

参考译文：这类报道很大程度上在于质疑这些有形事物的现实性——科技巨头提出的基础设施方案是否现实，数据中心本身是否可行，能源和水资源承诺是否真实，承诺的就业创造是否属实，最重要的是，这对现实生活中的普通人意味着什么。

"The AI boom has radically changed the physical presence of all of these tech companies in the physical world," says Blake Montgomery, the Guardian's US tech editor (who does wear a pair of leather-soled shoes). "These AI datacentres are some of the most massive and complex structures that humanity has created."

结构分析：第一句主干为 The AI boom has radically changed the physical presence；直接引语中 says 为谓语，Blake Montgomery 为主语，the Guardian's US tech editor 为同位语，who 引导非限制性定语从句修饰 editor。第二句为含 that 引导定语从句的复合句，修饰 structures。

参考译文：“人工智能热潮彻底改变了这些科技公司在现实世界中的实体存在，”《卫报》美国科技编辑布莱克·蒙哥马利（他确实穿一双皮鞋）说，“这些人工智能数据中心是人类创造的最庞大、最复杂的建筑之一。”

"It's very difficult for people to protest against technology or to make tangible their feelings about things that are transmitted over the internet. But while it's hard to protest against ChatGPT and what it means for your undergraduate kids' employment prospects, it's easier to protest against the infrastructure that facilitates its existence and how that infrastructure functions."

结构分析：第一句中 It 为形式主语，真正主语为两个并列不定式 to protest... 和 to make tangible...；that are transmitted over the internet 为定语从句修饰 things。第二句中 while 引导让步状语从句，主句为 it's easier to protest against...；that facilitates its existence 为定语从句修饰 infrastructure。

参考译文：“人们很难对技术本身提出抗议，也很难把通过互联网传播的事物带给他们的感受具体化。但是，虽然很难抗议 ChatGPT 及其对子女就业前景的影响，抗议促成其存在的基础设施以及该基础设施的运作方式却要容易得多。”

阅读理解练习

1. What is the passage mainly about?

  • A. The technical specifications of AI datacentres in Europe and the US.
  • B. How the Guardian's tech reporters have shifted from online monitoring to on-the-ground reporting.
  • C. The environmental damage caused by the rapid expansion of AI infrastructure.
  • D. A comparison between traditional journalism and modern tech journalism.

正确答案：B

解析：文章围绕《卫报》科技记者如何从关注屏幕和社交媒体趋势，转向实地调查数据中心、能源基础设施及其对社区和个人的真实影响展开，B 项最能概括主旨。

2. Why did Aisha Down go to Slough?

  • A. To interview tech executives about their investment plans.
  • B. To examine whether a planned AI complex was using renewable energy.
  • C. To experience the heat and noise generated by a major datacentre park.
  • D. To report on a protest against a new social media ban.

正确答案：C

解析：文中提到 Aisha 访问了欧洲最大的数据中心园区所在地斯劳，体验那里酷热的热岛效应，并描述了可听到的嗡嗡声，因此 C 项正确。

3. What can be inferred from Dan Milmo's remarks about public protests?

  • A. People are more concerned about abstract technology than its physical infrastructure.
  • B. Datacentre protests allow people to express their broader worries about AI in a concrete way.
  • C. Most protesters are parents worried about their children's university education.
  • D. Tech companies have successfully avoided all public criticism.

正确答案：B

解析：丹·米尔莫指出，虽然人们很难抗议 ChatGPT 这类抽象技术，但抗议促成其存在的基础设施要容易得多；因此数据中心抗议成为人们表达对科技和人工智能总体担忧的具体方式，B 项正确。

考研核心词汇表

单词 词性 中文释义 例句
shoe-leather reporting
/ˈʃuː ˌleðər rɪˈpɔːrtɪŋ/		 n. 实地采访；跑新闻 Journalists often use the term shoe-leather reporting to refer to the on-the-ground legwork.
legwork
/ˈleɡwɜːrk/		 n. 外勤工作；跑腿调查 ...the on-the-ground legwork that goes into covering certain stories.
colossal
/kəˈlɒsl/		 adj. 巨大的；庞大的 ...the physical world of colossal AI datacentres and social media harms.
underpin
/ˌʌndərˈpɪn/		 v. 支撑；巩固；构成基础 ...the datacentres and energy infrastructures that underpin AI.
misrepresent
/ˌmɪsreprɪˈzent/		 v. 歪曲；虚报；误导 ...an £8.2bn AI complex in rural Scotland has misrepresented its plans.
renewable
/rɪˈnjuːəbl/		 adj./n. 可再生的；可再生能源 ...to be powered entirely by on-site renewables.
constraint
/kənˈstreɪnt/		 n. 限制；约束 There are a lot of huge physical constraints and reality checks.
tangible
/ˈtændʒəbl/		 adj. 有形的；可触摸的；实实在在的 These physical, tangible things are what makes or sinks the AI boom.
feasible
/ˈfiːzəbl/		 adj. 可行的；行得通的 ...whether the datacentres themselves are feasible.
genuine
/ˈdʒenjuɪn/		 adj. 真实的；真诚的 ...whether the energy and water commitments are genuine.
intersection
/ˌɪntərˈsekʃn/		 n. 交叉；交集 ...the growing intersection of tech reporting with energy and environment stories.
trek
/trek/		 v./n. 长途跋涉；艰难行进 It involved Aisha trekking around Lanarkshire to sites...
outskirts
/ˈaʊtskɜːrts/		 n. 郊区；市郊 ...a four-acre site on the outskirts of London.
gleaming
/ˈɡliːmɪŋ/		 adj. 闪闪发光的；崭新的 ...due to house a gleaming AI supercomputer complex.
supercomputer
/ˈsuːpərkəmpjuːtər/		 n. 超级计算机 ...a gleaming AI supercomputer complex.
pull off
/pʊl ɒf/		 phr. 成功完成；实现 ...how difficult it is for tech companies to pull off these big infrastructure projects.
grid
/ɡrɪd/		 n. 电网；网格 ...the capacity of local electricity grids.
component
/kəmˈpoʊnənt/		 n. 组成部分；零部件 ...the availability of chips and other components.
carbon footprint
/ˈkɑːrbən ˈfʊtprɪnt/		 n. 碳足迹 ...the impact on tech companies' carbon footprints.
sustainability
/səˌsteɪnəˈbɪləti/		 n. 可持续性 ...carbon footprints and sustainability goals.
radically
/ˈrædɪkli/		 adv. 根本地；彻底地 The AI boom has radically changed the physical presence...
massive
/ˈmæsɪv/		 adj. 巨大的；大规模的 ...the most massive and complex structures that humanity has created.
complex
/ˈkɒmpleks/		 adj. 复杂的 ...massive and complex structures that humanity has created.
sheer
/ʃɪər/		 adj. 纯粹的；十足的；极其巨大的 The sheer scale of these developments is just one aspect...
physicality
/ˌfɪzɪˈkæləti/		 n. 实体性；物质性 ...one aspect of the physicality facing tech journalists today.
sensory
/ˈsensəri/		 adj. 感官的；感觉的 Another is the sensory assault.
assault
/əˈsɔːlt/		 n./v. 攻击；冲击 Another is the sensory assault.
sweltering
/ˈsweltərɪŋ/		 adj. 酷热的；热得难受的 ...to experience the sweltering heat-island effect.
vicinity
/vəˈsɪnəti/		 n. 附近；邻近地区 ...temperatures in the immediate vicinity of datacentres can increase...
mammoth
/ˈmæməθ/		 adj. 巨大的；庞大的 ...reporting on mammoth datacentres in Santa Clara.
essential
/ɪˈsenʃl/		 adj. 必不可少的；极其重要的 ...shoe-leather reporting is essential for covering the AI revolution.
grapple with
/ˈɡræpl wɪð/		 phr. 努力应对；努力理解 ...how children are grappling with AI.
recall
/rɪˈkɔːl/		 v. 回忆；回想起 Blake recalls sending tech reporter Dara Kerr to the Nevada desert.
stunning
/ˈstʌnɪŋ/		 adj. 令人惊叹的；极好的 ...for this visually stunning feature about their impact.
manifestation
/ˌmænɪfeˈsteɪʃn/		 n. 表现；显现 Protests against datacentres are becoming a kind of manifestation of voter and public concern.
facilitate
/fəˈsɪlɪteɪt/		 v. 促进；使便利 ...protest against the infrastructure that facilitates its existence.
flashpoint
/ˈflæʃpɔɪnt/		 n. 爆发点；导火索 ...these real-world flashpoints are part of a bigger shift in the tech landscape.
landscape
/ˈlændskeɪp/		 n. 格局；形势；地貌 ...a bigger shift in the tech landscape.
inquest
/ˈɪnkwest/		 n. 审讯；调查 ...the inquest into the death of Molly Russell.
self-harm
/ˌself ˈhɑːrm/		 n. 自残；自我伤害 ...died from an act of self-harm in 2017.
addictiveness
/əˈdɪktɪvnəs/		 n. 成瘾性 ...further cases on the horizon about the platforms' addictiveness.
perspective
/pərˈspektɪv/		 n. 视角；观点 ...how important their perspective was.
pervasive
/pərˈveɪsɪv/		 adj. 普遍的；无处不在的 ...because unreality has become so pervasive.

文章来源：The Guardian | 仅供学习交流使用

原文链接：https://www.theguardian.com/membership/2026/jul/12/tech-reporting-physical-world-ai-revolution-datacentres
